title: Listening - Part 3: The Price of Fast Fashion
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear an interview with a fashion designer, Sonia, and a supply chain expert, Thomas, discussing the environmental impact of the clothing industry. For questions 15–20, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which fits best.

extracts.context: An interview regarding the 'circular economy' in fashion and the psychology of consumer habits.
extracts.audio_script:
Interviewer: We buy sixty percent more clothes than we did fifteen years ago, but we keep them for half as long. Today we’re discussing the 'throwaway' culture with Sonia, a designer focused on upcycling, and Thomas, an expert in global logistics. Sonia, is the fashion industry finally waking up to its carbon footprint?

Sonia: [15] There’s a lot of talk about 'green' collections, but I fear much of it is just window dressing. The fundamental business model of fast fashion relies on volume and speed. [/15] You can't truly have 'sustainable' fashion if you’re still producing fifty-two micro-seasons a year. We need a complete shift from a linear model—take, make, waste—to a circular one where every garment is designed to be disassembled and reborn. 

Interviewer: Thomas, you look at the numbers. Is a circular economy even feasible given our global supply chains?

Thomas: [16] It’s incredibly complex. Most clothes today are made of blended fibers—like cotton mixed with polyester. Separating those at scale is a technological nightmare. [/16] However, the real bottleneck isn't technology; it’s the cost of labor. It’s currently cheaper to bury a truckload of unsold clothes in a landfill than it is to pay someone to sort, clean, and repair them. Without carbon taxes or legislation, the 'circular' dream remains a niche hobby for luxury brands.

Interviewer: Sonia, what about the consumer? Are we addicted to cheap prices?

Sonia: [17] We’ve been conditioned to think that a five-dollar T-shirt is normal. It isn't. That price is only possible through environmental degradation and poor labor conditions elsewhere. [/17] But there is a growing movement of 'fashion rebels'—mostly younger people—who are proud of wearing second-hand or mending their own clothes. For them, a visible repair is a badge of honor, a sign that they care about the story behind the garment.

Interviewer: Thomas, some brands are now offering 'rental' services. Is that a viable solution?

Thomas: [18] It works for high-end items like wedding dresses or tuxedos, but for everyday wear, the logistics are a disaster. The carbon emissions from constant shipping and the chemical impact of industrial dry-cleaning often outweigh the benefits of not buying a new item. [/18] We have to be careful that 'solutions' don't create new problems.

Interviewer: Is there a role for technology like 'digital passports' for clothes?

Sonia: [19] Absolutely. Imagine scanning a QR code on your jacket and seeing exactly where the wool was shorn, who sewed it, and how to recycle it. This radical transparency would make brands much more accountable. [/19] It would also help the second-hand market because you could prove the authenticity and quality of a garment years after it was first sold.

Interviewer: Final thoughts, Thomas? Will we see a 'fashion tax' in our lifetime?

Thomas: [20] I think it’s inevitable. Governments are starting to realize that the 'producer' must be responsible for the 'end of life' of their products. When brands have to pay for the cleanup, they will suddenly find a way to make clothes that last. [/20]

Interviewer: Sonia, Thomas, thank you.

questions.question: Sonia believes that current 'green' collections are often
options: A) the result of a genuine desire for change.
B) an effective way to reduce waste.
C) a superficial marketing tactic.
D) too expensive for the average consumer.
questions.explanation: She refers to them as 'window dressing' and says they don't address the core problem of high production volume.

questions.question: According to Thomas, why is recycling clothes difficult?
options: A) Consumers refuse to return their old clothes.
B) The process of separating mixed materials is too complex.
C) There are no landfills large enough to hold the waste.
D) Machines are unable to wash the clothes properly.
questions.explanation: He states that separating blended fibers like cotton and polyester at scale is a 'technological nightmare'.

questions.question: What is Sonia's opinion of 'fashion rebels'?
options: A) They are wasting their time by mending old clothes.
B) They represent a positive change in consumer attitude.
C) They are mostly motivated by a desire to save money.
D) They do not understand how the fashion industry works.
questions.explanation: She describes their visible repairs as a 'badge of honor' and a sign that they care about the garment's story.

questions.question: Thomas warns that clothing rental services
options: A) will eventually replace all retail shops.
B) are only profitable for the very wealthiest companies.
C) can have a surprisingly high environmental cost.
D) are unpopular because people dislike sharing clothes.
questions.explanation: He notes that shipping emissions and chemical cleaning often outweigh the benefits of renting instead of buying.

questions.question: Sonia suggests that 'digital passports' would
options: A) make it easier for people to travel with their clothes.
B) allow brands to track customers after a purchase.
C) improve the quality of materials used in factories.
D) increase the transparency and accountability of brands.
questions.explanation: She says scanning a QR code to see the source and labor behind a garment would make brands much more accountable.

questions.question: Thomas predicts that in the future, fashion brands will
options: A) stop producing clothes altogether.
B) be legally required to manage their own waste.
C) receive subsidies from the government for recycling.
D) move all their production back to Europe.
questions.explanation: He believes governments will make producers responsible for the 'end of life' of their products through taxes or laws.
